lt.gd: This bill requires a peace officer to make certain disclosures before testifying in a criminal case.
lt.hp: This bill:requires a peace officer to provide certain disclosures relating to potential Brady material to a prosecutor before the peace officer may testify in a criminal case;requires a prosecutor to provide a peace officer's disclosures to defense counsel in a criminal case; andincludes specific information that a peace officer must disclose.
lt.sa: Utah Code Sections Affected:ENACTS:53-25-1004, Utah Code Annotated 1953

leg.enact: Be it enacted by the Legislature of the state of Utah:

section.secline: Section 1. Section 53-25-1004 is enacted to read:

catline.bold: 53-25-1004. Testifying peace officer affirmative disclosure requirements related to Brady material.

subsection.amend: Except as provided in Subsection (1)(c), a peace officer may not be called to testify at a trial by a prosecutor in a criminal case unless the peace officer has provided a disclosure of information relating to potential Brady material to the prosecutor that complies with Subsection (1)(b).
The disclosure described in Subsection (1)(a) shall include:
the information described in Subsection (3);
any relevant contextual or explanatory information for a specific disclosure that indicates potential Brady material; and
the peace officer's signature and acknowledgment described in Subsection (4).

If a peace officer has provided a disclosure described in Subsection (1)(a) to a prosecutor within the 12 months before the day on which the peace officer is testifying at a trial in a different criminal case, the peace officer may testify at the trial if the peace officer provides the prosecutor for the criminal case:
a copy of the peace officer's previous disclosure described in Subsection (1)(a);
information regarding the case-specific disclosures described in Subsection (3)(j); and
updated information to any other information contained in the disclosure described in Subsection (1)(c)(i); or
an affirmation that there is no other updated information to provide to the disclosure described in Subsection (1)(c)(i).
A peace officer shall provide the disclosure described in Subsection (1)(a) or (1)(c) as soon as practicable to the prosecutor in the criminal case in which the peace officer will testify.
A prosecutor shall provide a disclosure received under Subsection (2)(a) to defense counsel as soon as practicable and in accordance with any applicable laws and rules.
The required disclosure described in Subsection (1)(a) shall include:
the peace officer's professional identifying information, including:
the peace officer's:
name;
employing law enforcement agency;
position; and
badge or identification number; and
the number of years the peace officer has worked in law enforcement;
information about the peace officer's employment history, including:
if the peace officer's law enforcement certification has ever been suspended, revoked, or denied;
if the peace officer:
is currently certified as a law enforcement officer in good standing;
has ever been terminated, suspended, demoted, or resigned in lieu of termination, from any law enforcement agency;
has ever been the subject of an internal affairs investigation; or
currently is under investigation by any entity for work-related misconduct; and
if a law enforcement agency has ever disciplined, reprimanded, or cautioned the peace officer for:
dishonesty, a false statement, or a misleading report;
misconduct involving the handling of evidence, tampering with evidence, failure to collect evidence, or failure to disclose evidence;
a constitutional violation;
an improper search or seizure;
bias or discrimination;
a body-worn camera violation;
excessive force;
a deficiency with writing a report; or
any other conduct affecting the peace officer's credibility;
information about the peace officer's criminal history, including if the peace officer:
has ever been arrested, charged, cited, or indicted for any criminal offense, excluding minor traffic or parking violations; or
has ever been granted immunity from prosecution or entered into a cooperation agreement with a prosecuting authority;
information about whether the peace officer is, or has ever been, a defendant in a civil lawsuit based on the peace officer's workplace conduct, including a lawsuit involving:
excessive force;
false arrest;
malicious prosecution; or
a civil rights violation;
information about the peace officer's knowledge of any previous adverse judicial finding relating to the peace officer, including whether a court:
has made an adverse credibility finding against the peace officer;
declined to credit the peace officer's testimony; or
found that the peace officer:
conducted a stop, search, or arrest that violated an individual's state or federal constitutional right;
suppressed evidence; or
provided false or misleading testimony;
information about:

the peace officer's knowledge of whether a prosecutor has ever declined to file a criminal charge, or dismissed a criminal charge, based on a concern about the peace officer's credibility or conduct; or
whether the peace officer has ever been notified that the peace officer is included in a Brady identification system;
information about the peace officer's workplace conduct, including whether the peace officer has, in a criminal investigation or case:
written a report, affidavit, or given testimony that included false, misleading, or embellished facts;
concealed, withheld, or failed to disclose exculpatory evidence, a witness statement, or information about a witness's credibility or bias;
claimed personal knowledge of information that the peace officer did not personally observe;
failed to follow legal requirements for:
evidence collection;
recording an interrogation;
obtaining a consent for a search; or
activating a body camera when required;
made a promise, offered a benefit, or provided an inducement to a witness in exchange for a statement or testimony that was not fully disclosed to the prosecutor; or
coerced the waiver of a suspect's constitutional rights;
information about the peace officer's potential substance abuse or impairment, including if the peace officer:
has ever been disciplined or treated for substance abuse; or
uses a medication, or has a condition, that could affect the peace officer's ability to accurately perceive, recall, or testify about events;

information about whether the peace officer has made an oral, written, or social media statement that could be understood by a reasonable person as demonstrating bias based on race, ethnicity, religion, gender, sexual orientation, or other legally protected characteristic that would affect the peace officer's credibility; and
information about the current criminal case in which the peace officer may be called to testify at trial by the prosecutor, including if the peace officer:

has a significant personal relationship with any of the following individuals involved in the current criminal case, beyond being an acquaintance or work colleague, that could influence the peace officer's testimony:
a victim;
a witness;
an attorney;
a judge; or
a defendant;
has made a:
derogatory statement toward the defendant; or
statement about the defendant that could be understood by a reasonable person as demonstrating the peace officer's personal bias, prejudice, or dislike of the defendant; or
has a professional or personal incentive to initiate or pursue the criminal case, such as meeting a quota, avoiding discipline, or winning an award.
A peace officer's disclosure provided to a prosecutor as described in Subsection (1)(a) or (1)(c) shall include the peace officer's:
acknowledgment, under penalty of perjury, that:
the peace officer's disclosures are complete and accurate to the best of the peace officer's knowledge; and
the peace officer understands that the peace officer has a continuing duty to:
update the peace officer's disclosures if a disclosure is no longer fully complete or accurate; and
disclose the updated information described in Subsection (4)(a)(ii)(A) to the prosecutor;
signature; and
the date of the peace officer's signature.

sectionText.amend: This bill takes effect on May 6, 2026.
